food_name: Chili Sambal in Jar (Sambal Bos Marah, Level 8 Spicy)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for jarred Indonesian sambal; closest parent is chili sauce food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chilli sauces and similar condiments; used as closest parent for this jarred sambal product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for jarred sambal chili condiment.

serving.natural_language_portion: One jar of Sambal Bos Marah contains 220 g of chili condiment; a typical single serving as a condiment is approximately 15–20 g (one tablespoon).

nutrient_source.title: Sambal (chili paste with oil and garlic) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate for chili-garlic-oil condiment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sambal Bos Marah exists in any approved primary database. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for chili pepper (raw, NDB 11819), garlic (raw, NDB 11215), and vegetable oil (NDB 04582), weighted to approximate a typical Indonesian sambal composition of approximately 60% chili, 15% garlic, 20% oil, and 5% salt/water. This is a medium-confidence estimate. Per-100g values reflect the blended condiment as consumed.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chili-based condiments and analogous low-carbohydrate vegetable pastes — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sambal Bos Marah or jarred Indonesian sambal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with chili-based condiments and low-carbohydrate vegetable pastes documented in the GI database. The high capsaicin and fibre content of chillies, combined with the low net carbohydrate content (~12 g/100 g), supports a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (15 × 12 × 0.15) / 100 ≈ 0.3, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~480 mg/100 g); condiment use can accumulate significant sodium intake
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sambal portion size to no more than one teaspoon (approximately 5 g) per meal to keep sodium contribution below 25 mg per serving, and should account for cumulative sodium from other condiments and sauces in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load per serving (~0.3); capsaicin may have modest insulin-sensitising effects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sambal Bos Marah has a low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load at typical condiment serving sizes, making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, total meal carbohydrate context and sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Oil content contributes fat calories; sodium level warrants portion control; capsaicin may have vasodilatory benefits at low doses
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use this sambal sparingly (one teaspoon per serving) to limit sodium and fat intake, while noting that capsaicin in chillies has been associated with modest anti-inflammatory and vasodilatory effects in peer-reviewed literature when consumed in small quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Bos Marah (Angry Boss Chili Jar) is a jarred Indonesian chili condiment produced by Smile Martabak at Funan, Singapore, and sold via field survey data. Each jar contains 220 g of a homemade-style sambal made from crushed fresh red chillies, garlic, and cooking oil, blended into a coarse, fiery paste. The product is marketed at spice level 8 out of 10, indicating an extremely high capsaicin heat level intended for experienced chili consumers. The texture is semi-chunky and oil-rich, with a sharp, pungent aroma from raw garlic and a deep red colour from the chillies. Nutritionally, the sambal is a moderate-energy condiment (~150 kcal/100 g) driven primarily by oil content (~10.5 g fat/100 g) and a moderate carbohydrate contribution from the chillies (~12 g/100 g). It is a meaningful source of vitamin C (~55 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (~3.5 g/100 g) from the chilli base. Sodium content is notable (~480 mg/100 g), which is relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is low (~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management when used in typical condiment portions. The product is vegan, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. It is used as a table condiment, dipping sauce, or flavour enhancer for rice dishes, martabak, grilled meats, and noodles in Indonesian and broader Southeast Asian culinary contexts.
common_user_queries: How spicy is Angry Boss Chili Jar?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Sambal Bos Marah or this specific commercial product exists in any approved primary database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for chili pepper, garlic, and vegetable oil, weighted to a typical Indonesian sambal formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chili-based condiments. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (chili pepper NDB 11819, garlic NDB 11215, vegetable oil NDB 04582) weighted to approximate a typical sambal composition (60% chili, 15% garlic, 20% oil, 5% salt/water). No exact approved-source entry exists for this product. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation — plant-based oil and fresh chillies contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA component data). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — all ingredients are plant-derived; confirmed zero per USDA component data). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived ingredients present). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based condiment with no fortification; confirmed zero per USDA component data). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 7. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chili-based condiments; no direct measurement exists for this product. 8. The product is sold as a 220 g jar at SGD 10.50 from Smile Martabak, Funan, Singapore via field survey data. 9. Spice level 8 indicates very high capsaicin content; not recommended for individuals with gastrointestinal sensitivity or irritable bowel syndrome. 10. FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 A00LC are used as closest parent ontology anchors; no exact codes exist for jarred Indonesian sambal in these ontologies.

Added Indonesian local name 'Sambal Bos Marah' to local_names.indonesian as required by G6 multilingual rule for Indonesian cuisine.
Estimated per_100g nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for chili pepper, garlic, and vegetable oil.
Assigned GI of 15 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for chili-based condiments.

Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases.
